demographics: I am 22 years old. I am female. I am White. I have never been married. I completed 4 years of college (bachelor's degree). My family income is $8,000 to $9,999. I am an independent, close to Democrat. I am liberal. I have no religious affiliation. I never attend religious services.

events.event_text: At 17, I joined a Black Lives Matter protest for the first time. It was eye-opening to hear personal stories from those affected by racial injustice. The unity and passion in the crowd inspired me to become more involved in social justice issues.

At 19, I moved into a dorm room in a predominantly Latinx neighborhood for my junior year of college. I quickly befriended my Puerto Rican roommate, Maria, and we learned so much about each other's cultures. This experience broadened my perspectives and deepened my commitment to racial and cultural understanding.

At 20, I joined my college's community service program and started working as a tutor for elementary school students from underserved neighborhoods. I saw firsthand the impacts of inequality, and it fueled my passion for social justice and public education reform.

At 18, I watched as my application for an internship at a prestigious law firm was rejected. I felt disheartened, as I had poured so much effort into my application. However, this rejection sparked my resolve to pursue social justice, and weeks later, I earned a position at a public interest law clinic that aligned more with my values.

At 21, I volunteered at a local community center, only to find out that the director had been embezzling funds, causing a dire situation for the programs and the people they served. It was devastating to see the impact of corruption firsthand.

At 19, I witnessed my best friend since childhood, who is Black, being racially profiled and harassed by the police while we were out one evening. It was a terrifying and eye-opening experience that reinforced my commitment to the Black Lives Matter movement.

compressed_summaries.summary: This person's activism began at 17, joining a Black Lives Matter protest, where they were inspired by personal stories of racial injustice and the unity of the crowd. At 19, they moved into a predominantly Latinx neighborhood, befriending a Puerto Rican roommate and broadening their cultural understanding. Despite an internship rejection at 18, they remained committed to social justice, securing a position at a public interest law clinic.

This person, at 20, began tutoring underserved students, sparking their passion for social justice and education reform. At 18, they were rejected from a prestigious internship, but this fueled their pursuit of social justice, leading them to a public interest law clinic. At 21, they witnessed corruption at a community center, deepening their understanding of inequality's impacts.

persona_card: This 22-year-old, college-educated, liberal White woman is unaffiliated with any religion and has little trust in institutions, having witnessed corruption firsthand. She is deeply committed to racial and social justice, attributing inequality to structural issues rather than individual failings, and is open and welcoming towards various outgroups. Despite her progressive views on most social issues, she maintains a slight lean towards traditional moral values, reflecting a nuanced worldview shaped by her experiences.
